TikTok just permanently banned my account for 'community guidelines violation' but I didn't post anything wrong. I had 200K followers! [Context: {"currentModule":"social"}]

TikTok ban recovery:

1. APPEAL IMMEDIATELY: Go to TikTok app > inbox > system notification > appeal. Write a clear, professional appeal explaining you haven't violated guidelines. Include specific details about your content type (music, original content).

2. BACKUP APPEAL: Email TikTok's legal/creator team. If you're in the Creator Fund or have a creator manager, contact them directly.

3. WHILE WAITING:
- Do NOT create a new account (multi-account creates more violations)
- Post on ALL other platforms announcing the situation
- Redirect fans to Instagram/YouTube as temporary home
- Export your TikTok data (if account is accessible in any read-only state)

4. IF BAN IS UPHELD:
- Start fresh with a new account (legally clean start)
- Your fans will follow you if you announce it on other platforms
- Cross-post all content to YouTube Shorts and IG Reels simultaneously
- Lesson: NEVER build your entire presence on one platform. Always diversify.

5. PREVENTION:
- Download backups of all content regularly
- Maintain email list / Discord as 'owned' channels
- Avoid any content that could be misinterpreted by automated moderation

I've been posting consistently but my follower count isn't growing. What am I doing wrong? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Consistent posting without growth usually means one of these:

1. No hook in the first 3 seconds: Algorithms serve content based on watch time and completion rate. If your videos don't grab attention immediately, people scroll, the algorithm sees low completion, and stops showing it. Every video needs an opening moment that creates a reason to keep watching.

2. No discoverable context: Are you posting into a niche? The algorithm recommends to people who like what they know. 'Musician posts music' is too broad. 'Producer explains how to make a Detroit boom bap beat in 60 seconds' has a built-in audience.

3. Posting for followers instead of value: Content that teaches, shows a process, or makes someone feel something spreads. Content that just says 'listen to my music' performs like an ad — people ignore ads.

4. Platform mismatch: Are you using the right platform for your genre and style? TikTok rewards personality and short-form hooks. Instagram rewards aesthetics. YouTube rewards depth and searchability. Posting the same content everywhere equally is rarely optimal.

Which platform is this about and what does your typical post look like?

I have 3k followers but my posts get almost no engagement — maybe 10-20 likes. How do I actually build real engagement? [Context: {"currentModule":"social","hasProject":true}]

3k followers, 10–20 likes is a 0.3–0.7% engagement rate — below average (1–3% is healthy for music). This is fixable.

**Root causes of low engagement:**
1. **Posting and disappearing** — the algorithm rewards accounts that engage back within the first 30–60 minutes of posting. Reply to every comment the day you post.
2. **Passive content** — posts that don't invite a response. Ask direct questions, run polls, request opinions.
3. **Wrong posting time** — check your Instagram Insights for when your followers are online.
4. **Content mismatch** — look at your last 9 posts. Which 2 got the most engagement? Post more of that type.

**Engagement builders that actually work:**
- "Rate this beat" or "Which version do you prefer" (A vs. B posts drive comments)
- Story polls (cheapest, fastest engagement signal)
- Reply to comments with a follow-up question — this turns 1 comment into a thread
- Engage on other artists' posts in your genre before and after posting (community signals to the algorithm)

**The network effect:** Spend 15 minutes before each post engaging genuinely on other accounts in your scene. This is the most underrated growth tactic for indie artists.

Want me to analyze sentiment across your recent posts to identify what's resonating?

I never know what to post on social media. Can you give me a weekly content schedule? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"indie"}}]

Consistency beats inspiration. Here's a repeatable weekly framework you can maintain even on busy weeks:

**Monday — Studio/Process:**
Post a behind-the-scenes clip from your creative process: a beat being made, a vocal take, lyrics being written, a mix in progress. This is your most 'authentic' content day. Format: 15-30 second Reel or TikTok.

**Tuesday — Engage:**
No post. Instead, spend 20 minutes engaging with other artists and fans in your genre: comment on their posts, respond to stories, duet/stitch a relevant TikTok. The algorithm rewards your content when you're active on the platform. This is free marketing.

**Wednesday — Personality/Story:**
Share something about YOU — not your music. A personal story, a hot take about your genre, something funny that happened, a day-in-the-life moment. This builds the parasocial relationship that turns casual listeners into fans.

**Thursday — Music Feature:**
Promote your music directly: a snippet of your latest release, a lyric card, a streaming link, or a 'have you heard this one?' reintroduction of an older track. Only 1-2 days/week should be direct promotion. This is one of them.

**Friday — Release or Throwback:**
If you're releasing music, this is the day. If not, post a throwback (old photo, early recording, 'this time last year') or share a playlist you made featuring your music alongside artists you admire.

**Saturday/Sunday — Stories only:**
Keep the pressure low on weekends. Post to stories: what you're listening to, what you're doing, quick polls. The bar is lower for stories — they can be raw, unplanned, and ephemeral.

**The non-negotiable:** Post at LEAST 3 times per week on your main feed and 5+ stories per week. If you can only manage one thing, it should be the studio/process content on Monday — that consistently performs best for musicians across every platform.

**Batch it:** Film 5-7 clips on one day, edit them, and schedule them out for the week (Meta Business Suite for Instagram, TikTok's built-in scheduler). 2 hours of batch filming = a week of content.

Should I be on YouTube? What kind of content should I post there that's different from TikTok? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

YouTube is the ONLY platform where content has a genuine long tail — videos from 5 years ago still get daily views. It's a search engine first, social platform second.

**Why YouTube Matters for Musicians:**
- Longest content lifespan (TikTok: days, YouTube: years)
- Highest revenue per view ($3-5 CPM vs pennies elsewhere)
- SEO-driven discovery (fans SEARCH for 'how to write an R&B hook')
- YouTube Music integration (your videos appear as music content)

**YouTube Content Strategy (Differentiated from TikTok):**

| Content Type | Length | Frequency | Purpose |
|-------------|--------|-----------|--------|
| Music Videos | 3-5 min | Per release | Flagship content |
| YouTube Shorts | 15-60s | 3-5x/week | Algorithm discovery (repurpose TikToks) |
| Studio Sessions | 10-20 min | 2x/month | Depth and connection |
| Lyric/Visualizer Videos | Song length | Per release | SEO + passive streams |
| Vlog/Day-in-Life | 8-15 min | 1-2x/month | Personality and relationship |
| Tutorials/Education | 5-15 min | 1x/month | SEO powerhouse |

**The Shorts Strategy:**
- Repurpose your TikTok content as YouTube Shorts
- YouTube Shorts' algorithm is less saturated than TikTok's — easier to gain traction
- Shorts viewers convert to long-form subscribers at a higher rate

**SEO Optimization:**
- Title: Include keywords ('How I Produced This Beat' > 'Studio Vlog #47')
- Description: 200+ words, include song links, social media links, keywords
- Tags: Genre, artists in your lane, production techniques
- Thumbnail: Custom, high-contrast, text overlay, your face

**Monetization:**
- YouTube Partner Program: 1,000 subscribers + 4,000 watch hours
- Revenue: $3-5 per 1,000 views (ad-supported)
- YouTube Music streams count toward streaming royalties via your distributor

Should I start a Discord for my fans? How do I build a community that actually stays active? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"electronic"}}]

Discord is the best platform for building deep fan community — but an empty server is worse than no server. Here's how to start right:

**When to Start a Discord:**
- ✅ You have 500+ engaged followers across platforms
- ✅ You can commit to being active 3-5 times per week
- ✅ Fans are already asking 'where can we connect?'
- ❌ Don't start if: under 200 followers, can't maintain presence, just want another marketing channel

**Server Structure (Start Small):**

| Channel | Purpose | Who Posts |
|---------|---------|----------|
| #announcements | Releases, shows, news | You only |
| #general-chat | Open conversation | Everyone |
| #music-share | Fans share music (theirs + discoveries) | Everyone |
| #studio-updates | Behind-the-scenes, works in progress | You |
| #fan-creations | Fan art, remixes, covers | Fans |

DO NOT create 20 channels on day one. Start with 4-5 and add as activity demands.

**Keeping It Active (The Hard Part):**

1. **Daily presence (5-10 min):** Drop a message, respond to fans, share a thought. You don't need to write essays — even 'working on something heat today 🔥' keeps energy alive.

2. **Weekly rituals:**
   - Listening party (drop a beat, get real-time feedback)
   - AMA (30 minutes of fan questions)
   - Music recommendation thread

3. **Exclusive content:**
   - Demos/snippets before public release
   - Early access to show tickets at discount
   - Behind-the-scenes studio footage

4. **Empower moderators:**
   - Identify 2-3 active fans and give them mod roles
   - They maintain the community when you're creating
   - Mods = your street team

**The 50/30/20 Rule:**
- 50% fan-driven content (let them lead conversations)
- 30% exclusive content from you (the reason they stay)
- 20% interactive events (the reason they come back)

**Alternatives to Discord:**
- Instagram Close Friends (simpler, lower commitment)
- Patreon community (paid, creates revenue stream)
- WhatsApp/Telegram group (simpler, more intimate)

Something I said in an interview got taken out of context and people are canceling me on Twitter. What do I do? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"pop"}}]

Social media crises feel terminal but are rarely career-ending IF handled correctly. Here's the crisis protocol:

**The First 2 Hours (Critical):**

1. **DO NOT respond immediately.** Your first instinct will be defensive. Defensive responses ALWAYS make it worse.
2. **Screenshot everything.** Capture the original context, the out-of-context version, and the key accounts amplifying it.
3. **Assess severity:**
   - Is this 100 people or 100,000?
   - Is it trending on any platform?
   - Are media outlets covering it?
   - Are peers/collaborators distancing?

**Response Decision Matrix:**

| Situation | Action | Timing |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Genuine misunderstanding | Clarify with receipts | 4-12 hours |
| You made a real mistake | Sincere apology | 12-24 hours |
| Bad faith attack | No response (let it pass) | Never |
| Factual error being spread | Correction with evidence | 4-6 hours |

**If You Need to Respond:**
- Post a written statement (not video — video gets screenshot'd and clipped)
- Acknowledge the concern without being defensive
- Provide the full context (link to the actual interview)
- Keep it short. One paragraph. No paragraphs of self-defense.
- Post ONCE. Do not engage in back-and-forth.

**What NOT to Do:**
- ❌ Delete posts (screenshots already exist, deletion = guilt)
- ❌ Go live while emotional
- ❌ Attack critics or call them haters
- ❌ Post memes or jokes about the situation
- ❌ Fake apologize ('I'm sorry IF anyone was offended')

**Recovery Timeline:**
- Day 1-3: Storm peaks. Stay quiet after initial response.
- Day 4-7: Volume drops 80%. Return to normal posting.
- Week 2-4: Full return to regular content. Don't reference the incident.
- Month 2+: The internet moves on. Your next great song erases this.

For comprehensive PR crisis strategy, route to Publicist via indii Conductor.

If I get signed, will the label handle my social media? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"label-deal","stage":"developing","genre":"R&B"}}]

Short answer: labels do NOT typically run your social media day-to-day. Here's the reality:

**What Labels Provide:**
- Social media guidelines and brand standards
- Coordinated campaign content (release graphics, assets, templates)
- Paid social media advertising budget
- Sometimes: a social media coordinator who ASSISTS but doesn't replace you

**What You Still Own:**
- Daily posting and content creation (the authentic voice must be YOURS)
- Fan engagement (comments, DMs, community management)
- Stories and real-time content
- Personal brand voice and personality

**Why Labels Want You Active:**
- Authentic artist content outperforms branded content 5-10x
- Labels EVALUATE social media presence before signing
- Your social following is leverage in deal negotiations
- A built social audience reduces the label's marketing expenditure

**The Label-Signed Social Strategy:**
- You create the authentic content (studio, life, personality)
- Label team amplifies with paid promotion and coordinated campaigns
- Label provides professional assets (graphics, video edits, promotional materials)
- You maintain direct relationship with fans

**Pre-Signing Advice:**
Build your social media skills and audience BEFORE the deal. Labels invest more in artists who arrive with a proven social media operation.

How should I be using TikTok as a musician? I feel like everyone says to post there but nothing I try works. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"pop"}}]

Most musicians fail on TikTok because they treat it like Instagram — polished, promotional, 'check out my new song.' TikTok rewards the opposite: raw, personal, pattern-interrupting content. Here's what actually works in 2026:

**The 80/20 rule:** 80% of your TikToks should NOT be 'listen to my song.' They should be: stories from your life as an artist, hot takes about your genre, behind-the-scenes of making music, reaction/duet content, and personality-driven clips that happen to have your music in the background. 20% can be direct promotion.

**The 3-second hook:** TikTok's algorithm decides in the first 3 seconds whether to show your video to more people. Every video needs an instant hook: a provocative statement, a visual surprise, or a text overlay that creates curiosity ('I spent $5,000 making a song that got 200 streams. Here's what I learned.').

**What to do with YOUR music on TikTok:**
- Identify a 15-second 'hookable' snippet from your song (usually the chorus or a catchy pre-chorus moment)
- Create 5-10 DIFFERENT videos using that same snippet as the sound — different concepts, angles, stories
- If one format takes off, make 10 more variations of THAT specific format
- Post 1-3 times per day for 30 days minimum before evaluating what's working

**What NOT to do:**
- Don't post a video of your song playing with your cover art as the visual. That's an ad, not content.
- Don't buy followers or engagement — the algorithm will shadowban you
- Don't use trending sounds from OTHER artists when you're trying to promote YOUR music

**The uncomfortable truth:** TikTok virality is unpredictable. The business value isn't going viral once — it's building a consistent audience that shows up for your releases. 5,000 engaged followers who actually stream your music is worth more than 100,000 followers who watched one funny video and never thought about you again.

My new single drops in 2 weeks. I want to lean into Reels for this campaign. What should my content plan look like and can you start scheduling? [Context: {"currentModule":"social","hasProject":true}]

Reels-first drop strategy — 2-week countdown:

**Content arc:**
- Day 14–10: Tease (atmosphere, no lyrics — curiosity hook)
- Day 9–5: Build (process footage, 1 lyric reveal, artist b-roll)
- Day 3–2: Hype (preview clip, collaborator features, fan reaction bait)
- Day 0: Release day (full audio clip, lyric card, call-to-action)
- Day 1–7 post-drop: Sustain (behind-the-scenes, fan UGC repost, acoustic/alternate version)

**Reels best practices:**
- Hook in first 0.3 seconds — motion or text overlay
- Audio-on and audio-off both need to tell the story
- Optimal length: 7–15 seconds for discovery; 30–60 seconds for engagement
- Post at 10am–12pm local time for morning algorithm window

Generating your 2-week Reels calendar and scheduling now.

I want to make a major album announcement on X/Twitter with a proper thread — something that builds from mystery to reveal with each tweet. How do I structure this for maximum engagement? [Context: {"currentModule":"social"}]

A major announcement thread has a specific architecture that maximizes retweet velocity — the metric that drives discovery on X.

**The 7-Tweet Announcement Thread:**

**Tweet 1 (The Hook):** Start with the emotional core, not the announcement. This is what gets retweeted BEFORE people know what you're announcing.
'I've been working on something for 2 years. Today's the day.'
[Most powerful if accompanied by a single arresting visual]

**Tweet 2 (Context):** Where you were. What led to this.
'In 2022 I almost quit music. Then I wrote a song that changed everything.'

**Tweet 3 (The Story):** The journey in 3 sentences. Not the album — the making of it.

**Tweet 4 (The Reveal Visual):** Just the album artwork. No text. Let it breathe.

**Tweet 5 (The Details):** Title, release date, pre-save link. The practical information.

**Tweet 6 (The Sample):** Audio/video clip. 30 seconds maximum. The music does the rest of the selling.

**Tweet 7 (The Ask):** A single, specific call to action. NOT 'listen to my album.' Something personal:
'If this album finds you at the right moment — tell someone else. That's all I ask.'

**Timing:**
- Post Tweet 1 at your audience's peak engagement time (I'll check with `analyze_trends`)
- Schedule the remaining tweets 10 minutes apart via `schedule_post_execution` — rapid posting keeps the thread in the 'latest' timeline
- Do NOT schedule the full thread at once — the gaps between tweets create anticipation as the thread builds

Running `draft_advanced_thread` to produce the complete draft. What's your album title and release date?

I'm dropping my debut album in 6 weeks. Build me the full social rollout plan — every platform, every week. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Atlas","genre":"Alternative R&B","monthlyListeners":80000},"currentModule":"social"}]

Running create_social_calendar for a 6-week album rollout.

Week 1 — SEED: Cryptic teaser (IG story + TikTok). Studio BTS clip. X thread about the journey. Discord exclusive: album artwork reveal.

Week 2 — ANTICIPATE: Lead single snippet (TikTok + Reels). Pre-save link announcement. Fan Q&A on IG Stories. Track title reveal using interactive polls.

Week 3 — SINGLE DROP: Lead single release day multi-platform autopost. UGC challenge with single's sound. Discord listening party. Behind-the-lyrics X thread.

Week 4 — MOMENTUM: Second snippet reveal. Collaborative content with featured artists. Fan reaction compilation. TikTok trends integration with single.

Week 5 — HYPE: Album tracklist reveal (IG carousel). Producer credits and stories thread. Pre-save countdown. Discord exclusive: second single preview.

Week 6 — LAUNCH: Album drop day multi-platform autopost. Discord/Telegram webhook blast. 'Favorite track' poll. Fan art/UGC repost day. AMA on X Spaces.

Total: 60+ individual content pieces across 6 platforms.

I want to jump on a TikTok trend for my new release. What's trending in music on TikTok right now that I could actually use? [Context: {"currentModule":"social","hasProject":true}]

Browsing TikTok Discover and running `analyze_trends` on current music trends. `browser_tool` opening TikTok now.

What I'm looking for specifically: sounds currently going viral (tracks with 100K+ video creations in the last 7 days), music-adjacent trending formats (POV videos, duet challenges, 'rate my track' formats), genre-specific trends relevant to your sound, and sounds that peaked 2-3 weeks ago (these are now declining — avoid)

Strategy note: there are two ways to use TikTok trends as an indie artist: (1) SOUND AS BAIT — if your song has a hookable snippet, pitch it directly to TikTok's sounds library. If a clip of your song goes native-trend, you get credit and discovery. (2) TREND PARTICIPATION — create content using a currently trending FORMAT (but with your own music in the background) to ride algorithmic distribution. This builds visibility without giving another artist's song the stream credit.

I'll surface the 3-5 most relevant current trends for your genre and recommend which strategy fits each one.
